question_text: A car travelling at 20 m/s brakes suddenly. Why does air resistance become more significant in stopping the car compared to when it was travelling at 5 m/s?
correct_answer: Air resistance increases with the square of velocity (speed). At higher speeds, the car pushes through more air molecules per second, creating a much larger opposing force.
explanation: Air resistance depends on velocity squared, meaning if you double your speed, air resistance increases by four times (2² = 4). At 20 m/s, the car experiences much more air resistance than at 5 m/s, so air resistance becomes a more significant force in stopping the car at higher speeds.
